posts.title: How did we get to Yuda.work?
posts.summary: A little overview of how this website was made!
posts.body:
Why this piece exists?

Everyone needs a website nowadays, at least that's what they tell me. So, around early 2026, I bought the domain Yuda.work as a bit of a birthday gift to myself. However, work on the website didn't start until when I discovered some stuff recently. 
If you want to look over the code yourself:
GitHub Link: https://github.com/bluemoon012905/Blueshellsite
Project Result Link: Yuda.work

How was it made?

Aside from all the youtuber blasting me with website builder/hosting services, where words like "essential for a business" and "online presence" are dropped, I've only spend very short amount of time thinking about website making. Sure, there are moments where I did some soul searching about whether wanting things and thus needing money made me a 1 person business, but I've never really see the true point of it. 

I had some assumption about websites:
  • It cost money to host! so if it doesn't give that back to me why should I make one?
  • It needs to be hosted somehow? Is this really worth learning to host a server for?
  • When was the last time you actually stumbled onto someone's personal website?
These however, were not true. Turns out, one of the best part about programming is you can make exactly what you need. 

A good friend showed me his own website that he put on his resume, which is by far the leading inspiration for me making this one. The website is under Heston.sh, though his website is much more polished. He's got a "this character has these stats" theme going on, except its him and his skills. During it being shown to me, he mentioned that GitHub can just host pages for you, for free. 

Of course, with not knowing what you are missing out on, there is not a whole lot of seeking. I didn't look into static hosting on GitHub pages until I had to a project on it. 

A good friend approached me about making a "multiplayer clock". Long story short, it was modifying an existing project, and it exists as a GitHub pages. There was nothing but one HTML file containing 1500 lines of uncommented code. That caused some problems for me, which I might do a ramble on in the future. However, as much it was annoying to figure out what is going on, it did teach me about static hosting. What it can do, what it can't. 

Several, much smaller projects later using static hosting, I was finally ready to start making this website.  

I've learned some cool stuff, so I've tried to keep the website as focused on the goal as possible: Hosting the contents I want to put on it. In fact, much of the "function" of the site is not even direction shown to the user. Of course, they are all very much present if you just open the repo on your local machine.

posts.title: Systems Class Zine
posts.summary: Turning a research-heavy assignment into a printed zine that was actually readable.
posts.body:
Prompt

The assignment asked for a systems map. I translated mine into a short zine so the relationships between sources, people, and constraints were easier to follow.

Structure

Each spread handled one layer of the system: actors, incentives, friction points, and possible interventions.

Takeaway

The project taught me that formatting is not decoration. The container changes how people understand the argument.

posts.title: Desert Notes
posts.summary: Short field notes from walking at dusk and paying attention on purpose.
posts.body:
Entry

The light was flat until the last ten minutes, then every pale rock started reflecting peach and silver at once.

I am trying to get better at recording details before they become opinions.

Reminder

Not every useful note needs to become content. Some of them only need to sharpen the next one.

posts.title: Notes on Voice
posts.summary: A draft about building a writing voice by collecting pressures instead of chasing style.
posts.body:
Starting point

I used to think voice was a finish you applied at the end. It feels closer to pressure now: what you notice, what you omit, what rhythm you trust, and how much risk you allow into the sentence.

Working rule

Write closer to the real temperature of the thought. Clean it up later, but do not cool it down too early.
